title: The Oasis Is Coming
synopsis: Facebook rebranded as Meta last month. The internet has responded with the energy it reserves for its favourite targets: mockery, memes, the comfor...
preview: Facebook rebranded as Meta last month. The internet has responded with the energy it reserves for its favourite targets: mockery, memes, the comfor...
content:
Facebook rebranded as Meta last month. The internet has responded with the energy it reserves for its favourite targets: mockery, memes, the comfortable satisfaction of watching a powerful entity make what appears to be a mistake. Zuckerberg's avatar, with its dead eyes and uncanny grin, has become the symbol of a pivot that most commentators regard as a desperate distraction from the company's mounting problems.

I am not laughing.

Not because I think Meta will succeed. It might. It might not. The specific company matters less than what the company is attempting, and what it is attempting is something I think is going to happen regardless of whether Meta is the one to build it.

The thesis: persistent, immersive, shared virtual worlds are coming. Not as a novelty. Not as a gaming peripheral. As infrastructure. As a platform layer that sits on top of the internet the way the internet sits on top of the physical network, and that becomes, over time, the primary environment in which a significant portion of human activity occurs.

I can hear the objections already. The hardware is clunky. The experiences are thin. The avatars look like corporate clip art. The latency is nauseating, literally. Nobody wants to wear a headset for eight hours. The technology is not ready.

All of this is true. None of it matters.

Google Glass was true in all the same ways. The hardware was awkward. The social dynamics were wrong. The battery life was terrible. The product was, by any reasonable assessment, a failure. And every single thing Google Glass tried to do, overlay digital information on the physical world, keep you connected without looking at a phone, provide ambient computing at the edge of your visual field, is now done by a smartphone that everyone carries and nobody thinks twice about. Glass was right about the future and wrong about the present. Being early was indistinguishable from being wrong, until the technology matured and the form factor changed and the thing that seemed absurd became ubiquitous.

The trajectory from text to images to video to real-time 3D environments is not a guess. It is a direction of travel with decades of consistent momentum. Compute is getting cheaper on a curve that shows no sign of flattening. Display technology is improving in resolution, field of view, and weight simultaneously. Tracking systems are becoming more precise. Haptics are developing. The individual components of immersive experience are each improving on their own curves, and the convergence of those curves points somewhere specific.

It points to Ready Player One. I know this sounds like the kind of breathless futurism I normally distrust. But the Oasis, Cline's term for the persistent virtual world that everyone inhabits, is what you get when you extend the current technology curves forward by ten or fifteen years. Not fifty. Not a century. A decade and a half, maybe less.

Zuckerberg sees the destination. I think he is probably right about that. What I am less sure about is whether he is building the right vehicle to get there.

There is a pattern in technology that I find instructive. The company that identifies a transformative platform shift is not always the company that wins the platform. Often the pioneer builds something that proves the concept, establishes the category, demonstrates that demand exists, and then gets displaced by a competitor that learns from the pioneer's mistakes and executes better.

MySpace proved that social networking was a thing people wanted. Facebook learned from MySpace and built something better. Friendster preceded both. The graveyard of technology is full of companies that were right about the future and wrong about the execution.

Zuckerberg knows this. He displaced MySpace. He understands, perhaps better than anyone, that being first does not mean being last. And yet he may be about to learn the lesson from the other side. Meta may spend tens of billions building the metaverse infrastructure, training the market, establishing the concept, and then watch as someone else builds the version that actually works.

The irony would be almost literary. The man who displaced Tom Anderson could become the Tom Anderson of virtual reality. The pioneer who proved the concept and lost the war to whoever builds the actual Oasis.

I do not know who that will be. It could be a company that does not yet exist. It could be Apple, whose hardware design instincts are better than Meta's. It could be an open-source collective, or a game studio, or something emerging from the intersection of AI and real-time rendering that nobody has anticipated.

What I believe is that the Oasis is coming, whoever builds it. And the questions that matter are not about the technology. They are about governance.

Who owns the Oasis? When a significant portion of human social, economic, and creative activity occurs in a virtual environment, the entity that controls that environment controls something more valuable than any physical real estate on earth. Not land. Experiential space. The environment in which human consciousness increasingly resides.

What are the rules? In a physical society, rules emerge from centuries of negotiation between the governed and the governing. Constitutions, laws, rights, the accumulated infrastructure of civilised coexistence. In a virtual world, the rules are written by the developer. The physics is programmed. The economy is designed. The rights of the inhabitants are whatever the creator decides they are.

Who has standing? If you build a life in a virtual world, a business, a community, a creative practice, relationships, and the platform changes the rules, what recourse do you have? In the physical world, you have courts, constitutions, the hard-won protections of centuries of political evolution. In a virtual world, you have the terms of service. Which can be changed at any time, by the company, without your consent.

These are not future questions. They are present questions wearing future clothes. The answers we develop now, while the technology is still young enough to be shaped, will determine whether the Oasis is a liberation or an enclosure. Whether it expands human freedom or concentrates power in fewer hands than ever before.

Everyone is mocking the metaverse. I think they are mocking the first draft. The final version will be extraordinary, and it will not be optional, and the company that builds it may not be the company that named itself after the concept.

The Oasis is coming. The question is not whether. It is who builds it, who owns it, and whether we think about governance before it arrives or after. History suggests we will think about it after, which means we will think about it too late.
